data.text:
INT: --15 with Mark Verstandig. You're talking about your period in Warsaw under false papers now, isn't it? 

MV: Mhm. So I went to these-- to these two brothers. They had a son. They were widowers. 

And they gave me a paper that I am employed by them to organize-- supervise and organize factories making [INAUDIBLE] for the-- making [INAUDIBLE] for the-- for the German army. And everybody, all people are-- are called upon to help me whenever I want to, whenever-- whenever I need them to help. Having such a paper, I came to the conclusion I needed-- it would be nice to have it translated into German. And I went with this paper to the government-- government general-- General Government office of-- headquarters of-- of-- of-- of-- of the-- of the-- of the German government in Warsaw. And I asked to have it translated. 

I asked, first of all, for people who-- who-- who can-- who can speak Polish, because I can't speak German. So they called me in. And I told them, look, I've got a paper like that. I have to organize. In Polish, I told them. I have to organize factories making [INAUDIBLE] artillery and so on. And I wanted to have it translated. So all these people clearly-- German ancestors should help me. 

And where will the [INAUDIBLE] stationed in Sandomierz. So he said to me, people coming from Sandomierz, they come. They always bring some sausage, some good things. 

So I said, so will be some more. I'll-- I'll bring-- I-- I-- I bought some. I-- I'll bring it tomorrow when it is ready. Said, don't forget. 

Next day, I came. I brought a kilo or more sausage and some butter. So I don't-- I'm going to bring a whole parcel. I brought it in. And the translation was ready. I paid them maybe a couple of sausage, or-- next to nothing. 

And I got the translation. I went out. I nearly fell over. 

I could see a document with the Government General-- head of-- headquarters of the-- of-- of-- of the German government in Warsaw. 'To whom it might concern,' and so on, translated. It was written with small letters-- 'translation' or something. Maybe it was not-- 'translated' somewhere. 

It looked like an original German document asking people to help me wherever I need help. The two brothers didn't believe their eyes, looking at this document. And with that, they sent me to Polaniec that I should start working over there. 

They told me where I should go, as I went to-- to this secretary of the amalgamated-- amalgamated villages-- offices. Like here, you have got these amalgamated city councils. And the ones over there-- the [NON-ENGLISH] There was a secretary of [NON-ENGLISH] of this council-- council of-- council of villages. 

I went over there. I-- and I wrote him a letter, another letter received not from these people-- from the two brothers. They knew the fellow-- that said he should help me, and so on and so on. And next day, I went to the-- the-- they-- they-- they arranged for me a host card, and so on. 

And I went and did my work. Did my work. I knew what I have to do, and so on. 

But what I wanted to tell you is-- is the same night that I came, there were a gathering of a few so-called intellectuals. There was a priest. And there were some other youngsters. And maybe you were-- maybe there was one who had-- who made some matriculation. Maybe-- maybe-- maybe, and maybe not. 

They were the intellectuals. And the whole talk was about Jews. They didn't stop talking on about Jews. 

They asked me to play bridge. But unfortunately, I couldn't play. I don't play-- I don't play cards. And I was reading a book. And they were talking and talking. 

And then, I remember, one said that there are quite a number of Jews who are trying to pass-- to pass-- to pass on as Poles. But you will always recognize a Jew. You will always-- when he-- when he lights a cigarette, you will see. A Jew is-- is lighting a cigarette by taking the match off-- from him, off. Whereas a Pole is lighting the cigarette by lighting the match towards him-- 

--covering-- covering the-- covering the light from the wind-- to him. At that stage, I thought-- I thought I will-- a little-- a little earlier, I thought it wouldn't be-- it wouldn't be the right place to make a speech like I did on the train. But at that moment, I told her, look, but I think you are wrong as far as the cigarettes is concerned. It's a difference between urban people and people in open villages. 

And-- in-- in-- urban people will never light the match towards them because sometimes some-- a spark can make a hole in the shirt or-- or-- or on-- or on their coat. They will light it against-- I mean, off. But in small villages, where in open-- the village is open and the wind is blowing, there is covering. This has nothing to do with Jews. That was the only time I took-- I-- they said, he makes a point. That was the only time I objected. 

And we were drinking a lot of Bimba. And I remember it was-- it was Saturday night. We were drinking a lot of Bimba. And I lied down. 

In the morning, they woke me up to go to church. I told them, look, I'm almost sick. I'm too tired. 

I drank too much. Apologize to the priest, the priest that was yesterday here-- apology. I can't come-- not come. 

Anyhow, on Monday, or on Tuesday-- a few days later. A few days later, one of the-- of the two brothers, he came along to Sandomierz, to this-- to this village. And I called him out. I told him, look, I'm sorry. I can't stay here anymore. 

They are talking about the Jews day and night and day and night. It is too stuffy for me. I just don't like it. 

And Jasinszki, I told him, told me, never stay in a small place. Never stay in a small place. And I told him I-- I was not going to-- even to stay overnight here. I'm going back to Warsaw now. 

And the same day, in the evening, I boarded a train. And I can tell you, I boarded the train. It was full of this-- of this smugglers and-- and black marketeers. And it was-- and you-- you could-- you-- you could smell-- you could smell the sausages. 

But I could feel fresh air, in comparison to the nice-- to-- to-- to the nice dwelling of this secretary there of all those combined-- combined villages. That was the answer to your-- to-- to your question whether it was common. The common-- the common conversation everywhere was about Jews. 

INT: Why do you think Poles were preoccupied with this question? Why do you think Poles were preoccupied? 

MV: Why? You know, here, I must tell you my observation. 

In the beginning, the intellectuals, the Polish intellectuals were-- were defending the Jews, because they thought the war will not take long. And as this solicitor, this barrister in-- in Warsaw said, I am for elimination of the Jews from the-- from the bar. But this is not our agenda now. Now there is a German occupation. And they are Polish citizens. 

You see, this were-- this was the approach of the Polish intellectuals when the war started. And that was going on until 1942, let's say March-- March or middle 1942. But when they looked around and they saw that the Jews were being killed, then they changed. 

They changed because they thought, now it is a good time to get rid of the Jews. And the blame will go that the Germans did it. And we will be our own masters. And here, we could see it was quite different. The intellectuals were the people who were feeding the population with the notion, now is the time to get rid of any minority, of-- of the Jewish minority in Poland. 

Now, the city dwellers were forward, because they already saw themselves occupying the Jewish houses and the Jewish dwellings and the Jewish shops. And as far as the villages are concerned, here we can see the people, the illiterate people, they were not keen on playing this game, this game of the intellectuals to get rid of the Jews. 

They were too clever to see that it is not going to happen like that. And if they can make, in the meantime, a few dollars by keeping a Jew and giving him shelter, they will do it. The only things they didn't were on the only-- the only fear they had was not the fear of the Germans, but the fear of the neighbor who was jealous, that vulture who is making money. And he-- he's-- and-- and they-- they thought that he is making big money. And he hasn't got a Jew to hide. 

If there would be a lot-- it would be 10 times as many middlemen to combine a Jew-- to connect a Jew seeking shelter with a peasant willing to give him shelter. 10 times, 20 times more Jews would have been killed. The only people who were against it were either the intellectuals and the city dwellers. 

And that has changed. From the beginning of the-- the beginning of war, they were defending the Jews. Because the Poles, their peasants were came in just to loot. It's a time to-- they-- they-- they can loot, but not to kill. But that changed in the meantime. 

And-- and it's a-- it's a matter of fact that they have were helped by the-- they were helped not only by these agitators from-- from the parties of the-- the well-known antisemitic parties. But they were agitated by the representatives of the-- of-- of the Polish government, by the Delegation, so-called Delegatura. And I've got a lot of documents to prove this point that say-- that-- that's pointed out that-- that the Jews, after the war, will not be able-- will not be-- will not allow-- will not be allowed to come back to their houses, to their dwellings, and to their businesses because they were occupied by the Polish people. 

And if they-- and if they-- and if they will endeavor to come-- if they try to come, they will be resisted by force, because that is not restitution that we will call it an invasion. The Jews coming back to their houses were-- were-- will be called invaders. And-- and these are documents which-- which are-- which-- which are originals of-- of the documents and photos of those documents are on the Yad Vashem. 

INT: This would seem to suggest that a certain proportion of Poles saw the Jews as being aliens in Poland. You said a-- was that a common phenomenon? 

MV: Yes. But you have-- you-- don't forget, they were alien to Poles for 1,000 years. They were living there for 1,000 years. 

The only thing was that they-- that-- that they were-- they were of a Jewish religion. They were Jewish religions. They considered themselves as a minority. And if you have got a minority, it does not necessarily mean you have to kill somebody. They were living here for-- for-- were living here for centuries. 

INT: At this time, at the middle of 1942, when you were in Warsaw, what was the fate of your parents? 

MV: What? 

INT: --was the fate of your parents? What happened to your parents? 

MV: What happened to my parents? Now, my father-- my father was killed in Mielec. My mother was with her daughter in-- in Mosciska. 

And later, they went from Mosciska to Kovel. I think I talked about it. They went to Kovel. Because in Mosciska, after they were thrown out from their house, as capitalists-- they had a little factory making-- a little factory making roof tiles. They went to Kovel having already Soviets passports, as-- as workers. So the only-- and my father was there too. 

No, I'm sorry. My mother was with my daughter in [INAUDIBLE]. My father was with my daughter in Mosciska. And they went from Mosciska to Kovel. And my mother stayed with my family, with her other daughter in [INAUDIBLE], because they changed. In the beginning, it was the-- it was-- it was different, but they changed. 

So the father was there. And later-- and there was my sister [INAUDIBLE]. And my sister [INAUDIBLE], Sarah, she-- she was in Krakow with her husband. And later, she came to Mielec, and she was in Mielec. And while she was in Mielec-- and my sister who was thrown out from [INAUDIBLE]-- Berta-- came to live with her. 

At that time, we received a note from my father, in [INAUDIBLE], that he would like to come back to Mielec. And we arranged, again, through a Jew called Mendel Bloch. He was a fixer. And he found a German taking him back through-- who offered to take him back from Kovel. I-- I think I talked about before-- 

INT: That's right. 

MV: --that-- that he didn't want money in advance. And he brought them safely, my sister [INAUDIBLE] and my father, brought them back to Mielec. And they stayed in Mielec with her family. It was two sisters. Well, there are three sisters, three sisters and a brother-in-law and my father. Now, the brother-in-law was taken to a-- to a labor camp or to a concentration camp when there was this Aktion. 

The two sisters went to Lubianka. And the one sister went to Lubianka too, but she died. She was-- she had tuberculosis. So she died natural deaths over there. The two sisters came back to Polaniec. 

And later, when hiding places were arranged for my family, for me, I had to go to Warsaw. My wife, to a safe house in the forest. And my aunt and her daughters went to a-- to a-- to a-- to a place in-- in Chorzelow. It is a village not far from Mielec. The middleman was a certain called-- a certain man called [INAUDIBLE] Valas. Now-- 

INT: And-- 

MV: --that, more or less-- 

INT: And your father was caught in the Aktion in-- in-- 

MV: My father was-- my father was killed among-- when the-- when there was this expulsion from Mielec and they took the Jews to this [INAUDIBLE], to where the hangars were. Outside the hangars, he was killed, together with 1,500 other Jews. And for my sisters, this merchant Valas found a place for-- for my aunt and his daughters. For my sisters, he couldn't find a place in Chorzelow. But together with a friend of his, they found a place in a-- in a-- in a-- in a-- in a town-- or close to a town called Jaslo. And they went over there. 

Later, in 1943, end of 1943-- Passover 1943. No, it was-- it was Easter time, 1943, when I heard and I got the news that where they were hidden, the Pole took them out-- roped them, took them out to the police, the Polish police. The-- and so-- and the Polish police shot them, killed them. 

INT: Getting back-- killed your mother. 

MV: My mother was with-- with her daughter, the eldest daughter in [INAUDIBLE]-- in [INAUDIBLE]. And what happened to her, I don't know. Must have happened to her the same as with the whole family over there. 

There was a sister, brother-in-law, his daughter. And his daughter had a fiance. And my mother-- they all were killed. I don't know what happened to them. I just don't know. 

INT: Sometime in mid '43? 

MV: Sometime in mid '42. End of-- maybe end of '42. It was already-- it could've been already autumn '42. 

INT: About the same time you were in Warsaw. What impressions did the city make on you as far as the conditions for Jews were concerned? 

MV: I didn't have any contact with Jews over there, while I was there. I lived with these two brothers. We used to go out shopping together, to the black market. 

And we used to go out-- we used to go out to a cafe on the [INAUDIBLE], a cafe called [INAUDIBLE] under the [INAUDIBLE]. And that-- something happened. It was a very nice cafe. There was a composer over there who played piano-- make a living. And one day, I was sitting with one of them in the cafe. 

And what he did, there were some serviettes in a glass, like that, you know. He took out the serviettes and started to draw the [INAUDIBLE] which we-- which we are producing. I have never been in the factory, you see-- which we are producing. Starts to tell me. 

And he said, it's good that you should have it. And he produced, let's say, a [INAUDIBLE], an artillery [INAUDIBLE] out of this [INAUDIBLE], number so and so, the measurements. He told me the measurements on such and such. And so he drew quite a number of-- quite a number of those things. And I put them into my pocket. 

It so happened that a few days later, I was walking the streets of Warsaw, and I saw a cafe. I thought, I have nothing to do. I'll go have a cup of coffee-- a-- a cup of tea. I preferred a cup of tea than a cup of coffee because the tea was [NON-ENGLISH]. It was herb-- herbal tea, a herbal tea. 

I was sitting and-- and drinking this herbal tea. And people were-- a lot of people were-- suddenly, I could see that-- that a few places just disappeared, that people are disappearing. And within-- within a second before, I'm-- I could see what's going on, Gestapo was already at the doors, all around. And ordered all the people to the wall. 

We stood all the people to the wall. And they asked-- there was one speaking Polish. He says, 'papier,' documents. He took the papers. He didn't look at them. He puts them into a bag. 

I thought, my god, it's no good, because he doesn't look at the documents. I was almost in the middle, maybe closer to the end. He came to me. 'Papier.' 

I took out my document, my-- my-- my gray Kennkarte. But the same time, I took out all those serviettes with the [INAUDIBLE] and let them go down. Because I knew that if he takes them, he will throw them into the bag. And they fell down. 

He took the card. He took the identity card, the ID, the gray ID. And then he saw that some papers are coming down. He grabbed it and looked. Military [INAUDIBLE] so and so, measurements. Maybe they got a spy. This is what I wanted. 

He called in immediately the-- look, what's-- what's going? And yet he asked me-- they asked me-- went, and what's that? And then I took out my document written in German, where it says, German-- Headquarters of the German-- of-- of the German Reich in Warsaw. That everybody should assist me and giving-- give-- to give me help, and so on. That I am such an important fellow, and so on. 

He looks at it. So he tells me, look, what are you doing? He says, there's no foreign currency here. 

Says-- I didn't know. I am-- I live in Sandomierz. I just came here because my people haven't been paid for the last six weeks. I came-- I came here to ask for money that I should go back to my-- then I saw a cafe. And I went in to have a cup of tea. And my cup of tea is standing over there and getting cold. 

He said, I'm sorry. And they conduct me-- they-- they took me back to my table and apologized [INAUDIBLE]. I was the only one, the only one-- also, I had a lot of luck. So only one not get-- not to be taken away. All the other people were taken away in a trolley-- in a-- in a-- in a-- in a-- in a-- in a lorry. 

INT: What name were you under in your Kennkarte? 

MV: The Kennkarte wasn't Polish. 

INT: What name were you using for your Kennkarte? 

MV: Ah. The name at that time-- Tomasz Dubula, I think. No, it was not Dubula. 

Yes, Tomasz Dubula. At that time, it was Thomas Dubula. Or Tomasz-- Tomasz-- sorry. Dubula, I came back. It was Tomasz Zajac. 

It was Tomasz Zajac. Dubula was the document I came back from-- from-- from the German part back to the-- from the Soviet part back to Germany. But that was-- the card issued by Jasinszki, [INAUDIBLE], was Tomasz Zajac. 

INT: How long were you in Warsaw for? 

MV: In Warsaw, it was almost till Christmas. 

INT: Christmas '42? 

MV: Christmas '42. 

INT: And during that time, did you see the ghetto at all outside? 

MV: No. I didn't go to the ghetto. I went-- I went close to the ghetto. And I saw this Jewish policeman coming out from the ghetto. And I got a million times more scared than-- than-- than-- than seeing Gestapo. 

INT: Why was that? Why? 

MV: Because I thought they may recognize me. The Gestapo didn't. 

They-- I-- I was scared of them. And I saw them in their boots and their uniforms. They gave me-- they-- they gave me a fright. I never-- I never went close to the Jewish ghetto. 

INT: Again. What-- what-- what sort of uniform did they have? 

MV: I don't remember. I don't remember it now. But it looks like a military uniform, but it was not a military uniform. 

It was a nicely cut uniform, because they had Jewish tailors in the ghetto, and they prefer-- they-- they made some very nice-- very nice uniforms. I wouldn't remember the color. But it was close to a military color, but not exactly like the Germans. A-- it was-- it was-- I don't remember that. 

INT: Do you have any memory-- 

MV: And-- and they had the-- and they had this-- this band, armband with a-- with a Star of David. But besides this, without the stars, they looked like a-- like-- like-- like Gestapo people. 

INT: Do you have any memory of the-- how-- how the walls looked? How the ghetto walls looked? 

MV: How the general walls? 

INT: The ghetto walls, what they looked like? 

MV: No, I don't, because I-- I don't-- I don't remember. I don't remember. Maybe-- surely, I saw them, but I wouldn't-- I wouldn't remember. 

INT: So were there any other important incidents during your stay in Warsaw? 

MV: Yes. It was-- first of all, it was one incident. I came-- later, when I was-- when I-- when I-- when I-- when I decided I'm not going to the villages, to Sandomierz, they made an arrangement for me-- they made-- they made some arrangements for me because they were afraid-- they were afraid of the-- of-- of the-- of the gatekeeper. Now, one of the brothers had a villa in [INAUDIBLE] on the line Warsaw-Otwock. 

So he said to me, look, I've got a villa, I've got a cottage in [INAUDIBLE], in the forest over there, as a-- a number of people of Warsaw used to have. I will go to rent another villa in [INAUDIBLE]. It's in the same line-- in [INAUDIBLE]. 

And you will stay with us for two days-- and in Otwock. You will stay with us for a few days. And then you'll go to [INAUDIBLE], back from work, going home for a day. Next day, you'll go to work, but you'll go to Otwock and stay there a day. And then you'll go to [INAUDIBLE] and stay a day. 

And then, after five, six days, you come back to Warsaw so that the gatekeeper could see that you are employed, that you are working. That was the arrangement. And it worked. 

One day, I went out of-- I-- that was [INAUDIBLE] in December. I went out from-- I came from Otwock. And I could-- I walked along the station. And I could hear heavy-- heavy-- heavy men walking behind me. 

I could hear heavy men walking. I looked around. And before I turned around, I had already-- he had already his-- his hand on my shoulder. And I turned around. And he said, 'You are a Jew.' 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue on next tape. 

MV: So you're--
